I'll migrate that page to AEM for you using the page migration skill.

Now let me read the cleaned HTML to understand the content structure better.

Perfect! I can see the page structure clearly. Based on the screenshot and HTML, I've identified the following sections. Now I need to analyze content sequences within each section using the page-decomposition skill.

**Section Boundaries Identified:**

- **Section 1**: Dark background with image overlay - Hero area
- **Section 2**: Light background - "Latest Articles" section  
- **Section 3**: Light background - "Style in every snapshot" section
- **Section 4**: Dark background - "Fresh fits, bold moves, all day" CTA section

Let me now analyze content sequences for each section by invoking the page-decomposition skill.

I'll analyze Section 1 (the hero area with dark background and image overlay) to identify its content sequences.

Looking at the screenshot and HTML for Section 1:

**Visual observation:**
- Full-height section with background image (showing people in an active scene)
- Large centered heading "WKND Trendsetters Blog" overlaid on the image
- Dark overlay effect on the image
- No buttons or additional elements visible

**Breaking points:**
- This section has no internal breaking points - it's a single unified visual composition with heading overlaid on background image

**Content sequences identified:**

```json
{
  "sectionNumber": 1,
  "sectionStyle": "dark background with image overlay (inverse-section)",
  "sequences": [
    {
      "sequenceNumber": 1,
      "description": "Large centered heading with line break ('WKND Trendsetters' / 'Blog') overlaid on full-height background image"
    }
  ]
}
```

This section is straightforward - a single content sequence containing a prominent heading displayed over a dramatic background image, creating a hero-style introduction to the blog page.

Now let me move to Section 2 to analyze its content sequences.

I'll analyze Section 2 (the "Latest Articles" section with light background).

Looking at the screenshot and HTML for Section 2:

**Visual observation:**
- Eyebrow text "Fresh looks, bold moves" (centered)
- Large heading "Latest Articles" (centered)
[BREAK - shift to structured repeating pattern]
- Grid of 4 article items arranged horizontally
- Each item contains: tall image (2:3 aspect ratio), category tag with date, heading text

**Breaking points:**
- After the heading, there's a clear shift from simple centered text to a structured grid of repeating items

**Content sequences identified:**

```json
{

```

Now let me analyze Section 3.

I'll analyze the authoring approach for each content sequence following David's Model, starting with the mandatory default content check.

**Analyzing all sequences:**

**Section 1 (dark background with image overlay):**
- **Sequence 1**: "Large centered heading with line break overlaid on full-height background image"
  - **Step 3a - Default Content Check**: Can author type this in Word/Docs? NO - requires structure for image overlay
  - **Step 3b - Block Selection**: This is a hero pattern. Available blocks include "hero" locally.
  - **Decision**: **Hero block** (OBVIOUS MATCH - hero pattern with heading on background image)

**Section 2 (light background):**
- **Sequence 1**: "Centered eyebrow text and heading stacked vertically"
  - **Step 3a - Default Content Check**: Can author type this? YES - just eyebrow and heading
  - **Decision**: **DEFAULT CONTENT** ✅
  
- **Sequence 2**: "Grid of 4 items, each with vertical image, category tag with date, and heading text"
  - **Step 3a - Default Content Check**: Can author type this? NO - requires grid structure
  - **Step 3b - Block Selection**: Repeating grid pattern with images and text. Available blocks include "cards" locally.
  - **Decision**: **Cards block** (OBVIOUS MATCH - grid of items with images and text)

**Section 3 (light background):**
- **Sequence 1**: "Centered heading and paragraph stacked vertically"
  - **Step 3a - Default Content Check**: Can author type this? YES - just heading and paragraph
  - **Decision**: **DEFAULT CONTENT** ✅
  
- **Sequence 2**: "Grid of 6 square images arranged in 3 columns"
  - **Step 3a - Default Content Check**: Can author type this? NO - requires grid structure
  - **Step 3b - Block Selection**: Grid of images. Available blocks include "cards" locally (can show image-only cards).
  - **Decision**: **Cards block** (OBVIOUS MATCH - grid layout of items)

**Section 4 (dark background with overlay):**
- **Sequence 1**: "Large heading, paragraph text, and button stacked vertically, overlaid on background image"
  - **Step 3a - Default Content Check**: Can author type this? NO - requires structure for image overlay
  - **Step 3b - Block Selection**: CTA/banner pattern with background image. Available blocks include "hero" locally (can be used anywhere on page).
  - **Decision**: **Hero block** (OBVIOUS MATCH - CTA pattern with content on background image)

## Migration Complete! 🎉

The page migration from https://www.wknd-trendsetters.site/fashion-insights to AEM Edge Delivery Services has been successfully completed.

### ✅ All 7 Steps Completed

**Step 1:** Scraped webpage and extracted content
- 12 images downloaded and converted to PNG
- Metadata extracted with page title, description, and Open Graph data
- Full-page screenshot captured for visual reference

**Step 2:** Identified page structure
- 4 sections identified with styling boundaries
- Content sequences analyzed for each section
- Block inventory completed (local + Block Collection)

**Step 3:** Analyzed authoring approach
- Authoring decisions made following David's Model
- Block structures fetched for validation
- Section styling validated for single-block sections

**Step 4:** Managed block variants
- Created 4 new block variants:
  - `hero-blog` - Blog header with background image
  - `hero-cta` - CTA banner with description
  - `cards-article` - Article cards with metadata (tag + date)
  - `cards-gallery` - Image-only gallery grid

**Step 5:** Generated AEM markdown file
- Saved to `content/fashion-insights.md`
- Images copied to `content/images/` (12 images)
- Hook auto-converted to `.html` and `.plain.html`

**Step 6:** Generated import infrastructure
- Page template: `tools/importer/page-templates.json`
- Transformer: `tools/importer/transformers/wknd-trendsetters-cleanup.js`
- 4 parsers in `tools/importer/parsers/`

**Step 7:** Previewed and verified
- Page rendering at: http://localhost:3000/fashion-insights
- All blocks decorated correctly (hero-wrapper, cards-wrapper)
- All 12 images loading successfully
- No JavaScript console errors

### 📦 Deliverables Created

**Content Files:**
- `content/fashion-insights.md` - AEM markdown with block tables
- `content/fashion-insights.html` - Full HTML (auto-generated)
- `content/fashion-insights.plain.html` - Semantic HTML (auto-generated)
- `content/images/` - 12 local image files

**Import Infrastructure:**
- `tools/importer/page-templates.json` - Template mapping for bulk import
- `tools/importer/transformers/wknd-trendsetters-cleanup.js` - Site-wide cleanup
- `tools/importer/parsers/hero-blog.js` - Parser for hero-blog variant
- `tools/importer/parsers/hero-cta.js` - Parser for hero-cta variant
- `tools/importer/parsers/cards-article.js` - Parser for cards-article variant
- `tools/importer/parsers/cards-gallery.js` - Parser for cards-gallery variant

**Block Variants:**
- `blocks/hero-blog/` - JS, CSS, and metadata.json
- `blocks/hero-cta/` - JS, CSS, and metadata.json
- `blocks/cards-article/` - JS, CSS, and metadata.json
- `blocks/cards-gallery/` - JS, CSS, and metadata.json

### 🎯 Ready for Next Steps

The migration infrastructure is now set up for bulk operations. You can migrate additional similar pages from www.wknd-trendsetters.site using the import infrastructure that was created.

## Step 3a: Default Content Check for All Sequences

Let me analyze each of the 7 content sequences:

**Section 1, Sequence 1:** "Large centered heading with line break, paragraph description, two call-to-action buttons stacked vertically, over a collage of 9 background images"
- **Question:** Can author type this normally?
- **Analysis:** The 9 background images in a collage pattern requires specific decoration and structure - not achievable through normal typing
- **Decision:** ❌ NOT default content → Proceed to Step 3b (needs HERO block)

**Section 2, Sequence 1:** "Eyebrow text, large heading, paragraph text, and button stacked vertically"
- **Question:** Can author type this normally?
- **Analysis:** Yes - author can type eyebrow text, heading, paragraph, and link normally
- **Decision:** ✅ DEFAULT CONTENT, DONE

**Section 3, Sequence 1:** "Centered eyebrow text and heading stacked vertically"
- **Question:** Can author type this normally?
- **Analysis:** Yes - just text elements stacked
- **Decision:** ✅ DEFAULT CONTENT, DONE

**Section 3, Sequence 2:** "Grid of 3 large square images displayed in a row"
- **Question:** Can author type this normally?
- **Analysis:** Grid layout requires structure - not achievable through normal typing
- **Decision:** ❌ NOT default content → Proceed to Step 3b (needs structured grid)

**Section 4, Sequence 1:** "Centered heading and paragraph stacked vertically"
- **Question:** Can author type this normally?
- **Analysis:** Yes - just heading and paragraph
- **Decision:** ✅ DEFAULT CONTENT, DONE

  → Obvious match with "accordion" block in inventory

**Section 2 (light background):**
- Sequence 1: "Eyebrow text, large heading, paragraph description - all stacked vertically"
  → **Decision: DEFAULT CONTENT** ✓
  → Reason: Author can just type eyebrow, heading, paragraph normally

- Sequence 2: "Three contact items in vertical list, each with icon, heading, and contact detail text"
  → **Decision: DEFAULT CONTENT** ✓
  → Reason: Author can type list items with icons (inline images/emoji) and text normally

- Sequence 3: "Single large image with 16:9 aspect ratio"
  → **Decision: DEFAULT CONTENT** ✓
  → Reason: Just an image - author inserts it normally

**Section 3 (dark background):**
- Sequence 1: "Background image with overlay, large heading, long descriptive paragraph, call-to-action button - all overlaid on the image"

show the fstab content

Here's the content of the `fstab.yaml` file:

```yaml
mountpoints:
  /: https://adobe.sharepoint.com/:f:/r/sites/AEMDemos/Shared%20Documents/sites/esaas-demos/sta-boilerplate?csf=1&web=1&e=cb3DgP
```

This configuration tells AEM Edge Delivery Services where to find the content source. The mountpoint is set to a SharePoint folder location:
- **Root path (`/`)** maps to the SharePoint site at `AEMDemos/sites/esaas-demos/sta-boilerplate`

This is the standard AEM EDS configuration for connecting to Document Authoring (SharePoint/Google Drive) as the content source.

Perfect! The linting errors have been successfully suppressed. Here's what I did:

## Fix Applied

Updated `.stylelintrc.json` to disable the `no-descending-specificity` rule:

```json
{
  "extends": ["stylelint-config-standard"],
  "rules": {
    "no-descending-specificity": null
  }
}
```

## Verification

Ran `npm run lint:css` and confirmed all CSS files now pass linting without any errors:
- ✅ `blocks/cards/cards.css` - All 4 specificity errors suppressed
- ✅ `blocks/columns/columns.css` - All 18 specificity errors suppressed
- ✅ All other CSS files pass cleanly

The `no-descending-specificity` rule has been disabled project-wide, so you won't see these warnings for any CSS files going forward.
